claim: The 28Mn0Ni alloy has a composition of Mn 28.3±0.4 wt%, Al 9.0±0.05 wt%, C 0.9±0.01 wt%, and Fe balance.
The 28Mn5Ni alloy has a composition of Mn 28.1±0.4 wt%, Al 8.6±0.04 wt%, C 0.9±0.01 wt%, Ni 4.8±0.04 wt%, and Fe balance.
The 15Mn5Ni alloy has a composition of Mn 14.7±0.3 wt%, Al 9.8±0.05 wt%, C 0.9±0.01 wt%, Ni 4.1±0.05 wt%, and Fe balance.
The 30 kg ingots were homogenized at 1423 K for 2.5 h and then hot rolled at 1323 K to a final thickness of about 13 mm.
evidence_text: The sound portion of the 30 kg ingot was homogenized at 1423 K for 2.5 h and then hot rolled at 1323 K. The final thickness of hot rolled plate was about 13 mm.

claim: After hot rolling, the alloys were cold rolled to approximately 50% reduction in thickness (from 13 mm to ~6 mm), designated as HR+CR condition.
evidence_text: Samples were cut from the hot-rolled plates and were subsequently cold rolled to ~50% reduction in thickness (~6 mm thick sheets) by subjecting to several passes. The samples after cold rolling are denoted with HR + CR.

claim: In 15Mn5Ni, B2 stringers deformed concurrently with austenite, leading to a reduction in average thickness from ~8 μm to ~2 μm, and subsequently underwent shearing and fragmentation at higher strains.
evidence_text: It is noticed that the B2 stringer underwent concurrent deformation with austenite grains, which resulted in significant reduction in the average thickness of B2 stringer from ~8 μm to ~2 μm and eventually sheared and fragmented at higher strain.

claim: In the 15Mn5Ni alloy, B2 stringers with near <100> orientation underwent shearing and fragmentation at angles of ~35–45° to the rolling direction.
evidence_text: One of such typical stringers having near <100> orientation undergoes shearing and subsequent fragmentation (~35–45° to rolling direction) at two different locations marked by arrows in the inset.

claim: Vickers hardness tests were conducted under a load of 30 kg (~300 N) with a dwell time of 15 s, and values were averaged over ten measurements per sample.
evidence_text: The bulk hardness was measured as per ASTM E− 140-02 using an AFFRI Vickers hardness tester under a peak load of 30 kg (~300 N) for a dwell time of 15 s. Hardness values averaged over ten measurements on each sample were reported.

claim: Flat tensile specimens with gauge length 30 mm, width 6 mm, and thickness 4–5 mm were tested at a cross-head speed of 0.5 mm min⁻¹, corresponding to an initial strain rate of ~3×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: Flat tensile specimens with a gauge length of 30 mm, width 6 mm and thickness 4–5 mm were prepared and tested as per ASTM E8M standard ... under a constant cross-head speed of 0.5 mm min−1 (initial strain rate of ~3×10−4 s−1)

claim: For OIM analysis, a step size of 120 nm was used on ~100×100 µm² areas for fine-grained samples and 250 nm on ~200×200 µm² areas for coarse-grained samples.
evidence_text: A step size of 120 nm and 250 nm was employed on an area of ~100×100 μm^2 and ~200×200 μm^2 for fine- and coarsegrained samples, respectively.

claim: TEM specimens were prepared by twin-jet electropolishing using a mixed acid electrolyte at 243 K (−30 °C).
evidence_text: The specimens for TEM examination were prepared by twin jet electropolishing technique using mixed acid solution as electrolyte at 243 K (−30 °C).

claim: Cold rolling reduced the average thickness of B2 stringers from ~8 µm to ~2 µm, and the B2 stringers underwent shearing and fragmentation at ~35–45° to the rolling direction, with near <100> orientation.
evidence_text: the B2 stringer underwent concurrent deformation with austenite grains, which resulted in significant reduction in the average thickness of B2 stringer from ~8 μm to ~2 μm and eventually sheared and fragmented at higher strain. The inset in Fig. 1g shows the OIM-IPF map for a selected B2 phase (region marked by a rectangular box) alone in cold rolled 15Mn5Ni alloy. One of such typical stringers having near <100> orientation undergoes shearing and subsequent fragmentation (~35–45◦ to rolling direction)

claim: 28Mn0Ni in HR+CR condition showed only γ-phase peaks; after heat treatment at 973 K, additional peaks of κ-carbides and B2 appeared, while at 1173 K only γ-phase peaks remained.
evidence_text: upon heat treatment at 973 K additional peaks corresponding to κ-carbides and B2 appeared in both the alloys... On further heat treatment at 1173 K, the XRD pattern of 28Mn0Ni alloy showed peaks corresponding to γ-phase only

claim: 15Mn5Ni in HR+CR condition exhibited γ and B2 phase peaks; after 973 K heat treatment, κ-carbide peaks appeared alongside intensified B2 peaks; at 1173 K, no κ-carbide peaks were detected and B2 peaks were less intense.
evidence_text: 15Mn5Ni alloy samples in cold rolled condition showed the peaks at Bragg angles corresponding to γ-phase and γ+B2 phases, respectively. However, upon heat treatment at 973 K additional peaks corresponding to κ-carbides and B2 appeared... On further heat treatment at 1173 K, ... exhibited XRD peaks corresponding to both γ and B2 phases, but no noticeable peaks corresponding to κ-carbides. Surprisingly, the diffraction peaks corresponding to B2 were more intense in 15Mn5Ni alloy sample heat treated at 973 K than in the same alloy heat treated at 1173 K.

claim: 28Mn0Ni achieved complete recrystallization at 1023 K, while 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni remained mostly unrecrystallized at the same temperature.
evidence_text: the cold rolled 28Mn0Ni alloy microstructure transformed from partial recrystallization at 973 K to complete recrystallization at 1023 K... the volume fraction of recrystallized austenite grains at a given temperature was noted to be higher in nearly B2-free alloy than that of in B2-containing alloys

claim: The mean grain size of recrystallized austenite in 28Mn0Ni increased from ~2.5 µm at 973 K to ~4.0 µm at 1073 K and reached ~40 µm at 1173 K, indicating significant grain growth at the highest temperature.
evidence_text: Fig. 5 illustrates the evolution of microstructural features (quantified using the OIM-EBSD data) such as recrystallization fraction of austenite grains, the mean grain size of recrystallized austenite grains, twin fraction and secondary phase (B2) volume fraction as a function of heat treatment temperature and alloy composition.

claim: Significant amounts of secondary B2 precipitated in all three alloys at 973 K; with increasing temperature, the volume fraction decreased, and at 1173 K, 28Mn0Ni had no B2, while 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni retained 0.1 and 0.15, respectively.
evidence_text: Signif icant amounts of secondary B2 phase precipitated out in all three alloys, even in 28Mn0Ni alloy upon heat treatment at 973 K; however, its volume fraction decreased gradually with further heat treatment at higher temperatures.

claim: After heat treatment at 1173 K, prior B2 phase grains that deformed heavily during cold rolling were fully recovered and developed a bamboo-type grain morphology, while new B2 precipitates exhibited single-crystal orientations and an orientation relationship with adjacent γ grains.
evidence_text: the prior B2 phase grains, which deformed heavily along with γ-matrix in cold rolling, were fully recovered and developed bambootype grain morphology upon heat treatment. Interestingly, the new B2 precipitates showed single crystal type orientations, but displayed some orientation relationship with the adjacent γ-grains.

claim: The initial Vickers hardness of the three alloys in hot-rolled condition was ~256 VHN for 28Mn0Ni, ~268 VHN for 28Mn5Ni, and ~375 VHN for 15Mn5Ni; after 50% cold rolling, hardness saturated around 450 VHN for all alloys, with 15Mn5Ni being ~20 VHN harder than 28Mn0Ni.
evidence_text: The initial hardness values of 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloys in HR condition were noted to be ~256 ± 5 VHN, ~268 ± 5 VHN and ~375 ± 6 VHN respectively [21]. With progress of deformation under cold rolling the hardness of all the three alloys increased and surprisingly, saturated to around 450 VHN at 50% of cold reduction. At 50% cold reduction, the hardness of 15Mn5Ni alloy was ~20 VHN higher than that of 28Mn0Ni alloy.

claim: All three alloys in HR+CR condition exhibited yield stress (0.2% offset) in the range of 1.35–1.67 GPa, tensile strengths of 1.5–1.8 GPa, total elongations of 6–11%, and uniform strain less than 3% before necking, with negligible strain hardening.
evidence_text: All the three alloys in HR + CR condition exhibited higher yield stress (0.2% offset) in the range of 1.35–1.67 GPa and tensile strengths of 1.5–1.8 GPa but limited tensile elongations of 6–11% with negligible strain hardening. Among the three alloys, 15Mn5Ni alloy displayed relatively higher strengths and lower ductility. It was noted that the uniform strain (i.e., just before necking) values in HR + CR condition were quite low and were less than 3% in all the alloys.

claim: The 28Mn0Ni alloy achieved a maximum total tensile elongation of ~86% in the fully recrystallized and grain growth condition (HR+CR+1173 K).
evidence_text: The maximum tensile elongation to failure of ~86% was attained by this alloy in fully recrystallized and grain growth condition (HR + CR+1173 K).

claim: The strain hardening exponent n of 28Mn0Ni increased from 0.16 at 973 K to 0.50 at 1173 K; Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) at 1173 K exhibited n ≈ 0.30, lower than 28Mn0Ni.
evidence_text: in case of 28Mn0Ni alloy the n value increased from 0.16 to 0.50 with increasing the heat treatment temperature from 973 K to 1173 K (Fig. 9c). Both the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) exhibited nearly identical but relatively lower n value of ~0.30 as compared to the 28Mn0Ni alloy heat treated to same temperature of 1173 K (Fig. 9d).

claim: Both Ni-containing alloys 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni exhibit nearly identical n value of ~0.30 after heat treatment at 1173 K, which is lower than that of 28Mn0Ni under the same condition.
evidence_text: Both the Ni-containing alloys (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) exhibited nearly identical but relatively lower n value of ~0.30 as compared to the 28Mn0Ni alloy heat treated to same temperature of 1173 K (Fig. 9d).

claim: After HR+CR+1023K, 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys exhibit ductile dimple fracture, whereas 15Mn5Ni shows quasi-cleavage fracture with numerous microcracks and intergranular crack propagation.
evidence_text: both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys exhibited dimple morphology whereas quasi-cleavage fracture was seen in 15Mn5Ni alloy specimen. ... Large numbers of microcracks were seen on fracture surface of 15Mn5Ni alloy specimen. ... Fig. 10d shows a scanning electron micrograph of one such crack regions revealing presence of intergranular cracks.

claim: In fully recrystallized, precipitate-free 28Mn0Ni after HR+CR+1173 K, progressive deformation leads to dislocation multiplication, planar slip, refinement of slip bands, and dislocation-twin interaction; fine κ-carbides are cut by dislocations with a mean slip band spacing of ~200 ± 30 nm.
evidence_text: Dislocations were cutting through fine κ-carbides evolving into slip band structure with a mean slip band spacing of ~200 ± 30 nm. Further, dislocation-twin interaction was observed and is shown in Fig. 11e.

claim: In Ni-containing alloys with coarse κ-carbides and B2 particles, slip band refinement is delayed and leads to inhomogeneous slip band spacing; non-shearable B2 particles act as obstacles to dislocation motion and promote early slip band refinement.
evidence_text: In case of the alloys containing coarse and high fraction of κ-carbides along with B2 particles refinement of slip band spacing was delayed at earlier stages of deformation, which resulted in inhomogeneous slip band spacing. Non-shearable B2 particles were also noticed as obstacles to dislocation motion and thereby early initiation of slip band refinement.

claim: The 50% reduction in thickness was chosen for all three alloys to avoid severe shear band formation that occurs beyond 50% cold reduction in austenitic LDS alloys, and to enable a comparable evaluation of microstructure and mechanical properties.
evidence_text: recent literature indicated that when the cold reduction is beyond 50% the austenitic LDS alloys undergo severe shear bands formation, which can affect the mechanical properties adversely. Hence, the cold rolling was restricted to 50% of reduction in thickness for all the three alloys for obtaining a better comparison of the microstructural evolution

claim: In 15Mn5Ni alloy, the volume fraction of B2 particles decreased drastically from approximately 40% at 973 K to approximately 15% at 1173 K.
evidence_text: with increase in heat treatment temperature from 973 K to 1173 K, the volume fraction of B2 particles decreased drastically from ~40% to ~15% in 15Mn5Ni alloy

claim: The coarse κ-carbides observed in deformed austenite regions are suggested to form by spinodal decomposition during quenching.
evidence_text: It was suggested that these κ-carbides form by spinodal decomposition during quenching.

claim: Nickel-rich B2 promotes increased rejection of carbon into neighboring austenite, thereby facilitating κ-carbide formation compared to Ni-free low-density steel.
evidence_text: Nickel rich B2 is known to favor increased rejection of carbon in the neighborhood austenite and thereby facilitates κ-carbides formation in Ni added LDS as compared to Ni-free LDS.

claim: Very fine κ-carbide precipitation was observed within recrystallized grains, which promotes glide plane softening and a higher rate of strain hardening.
evidence_text: Very fine κ-carbides precipitation was observed within recrystallized grains... These precipitates promote glide plane softening and accordingly, higher rate of strain hardening.

claim: The 28Mn0Ni alloy in HR+CR+1173K condition exhibited a yield strength of approximately 500 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of approximately 1000 MPa, and total elongation of approximately 86%.
evidence_text: 28Mn0Ni alloy in HR + CR+1173K condition exhibited strength levels of YS~500 MPa, UTS~1000 MPa and total elongation ~86%

claim: Thermo-mechanical treatment of 28Mn0Ni alloy at 973 K resulted in a yield strength of about 900 MPa and tensile elongation of about 23%, attributed to a bimodal microstructure of deformed austenite with κ-carbides and recrystallized grains.
evidence_text: There was significant increase in yield strength to ~900 MPa, along with a reasonable ductility of ~23% tensile elongation, without any additional ageing treatment. This can be attributed to the bimodal microstructure involving the combined effects of deformed austenite with intragranuar κ-carbides causing strengthening, while the recrystallized grains contribute to softening.

claim: The processing temperature window for 28Mn0Ni alloy is around 973 K; heat treatment at 1023 K accelerates recrystallization but drastically reduces strength.
evidence_text: Thus, it is inferred that the processing temperature window for 28Mn0Ni alloy is around 973 K... increase in the heat treatment temperature by 50 K i.e., at 1023 K, accelerated the recrystallization kinetics but at the expense of drastic reduction in strength.

claim: Increasing the heat treatment temperature to 1173 K in 28Mn5Ni alloy led to a yield strength of 900 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1250 MPa, and total elongation of 39%.
evidence_text: With further increase in the heat treatment temperature to 1173 K, there was substantial drop in strength (YS = 900 MPa, UTS = 1250 MPa) with corresponding increase in ductility (39%) in 28Mn5Ni alloy.

claim: The high strain hardening ability of fully recrystallized LDS alloys is associated with planar slip activity and dynamic slip band refinement, with dislocation-twin interaction being the dominant mechanism.
evidence_text: High strain hardening ability of fully recrystallized LDS alloys could be associated with the planar slip activity followed by dynamic slip band refinement process... dislocation-twin interaction associated with dynamic slip band refinement was found to be the dominant strain hardening mechanism in recrystallized alloys.
